sentiment.rationale: Jerusalem sits desolate like a widow; the poet mourns her fallen state, her sins, and the departure of all comfort.
God's fierce anger has destroyed Zion's strongholds; the prophet weeps at the devastation and calls Jerusalem to cry out.
The poet descends into the depths of affliction but finds hope in God's mercies which are new every morning.
A graphic description of the horrors of the siege—starvation, suffering of children, and the punishment of Zion's iniquity.
A communal prayer of lament recounting the people's suffering under foreign oppression, closing with a plea for restoration.

summaries.summary: Jerusalem sits desolate and weeping, mourning like a widow; her former glory is gone, her enemies prosper, and she acknowledges that the Lord has afflicted her for her many transgressions.
The Lord in his anger has destroyed the strongholds of Judah and cast down her beauty; the prophet weeps at the ruin of the daughter of Zion and calls her to pour out her heart before God.
The poet descends into deep affliction and despair but finds renewed hope in God's compassions which are new every morning, and calls for self-examination and repentance.
A vivid account of the horrors of Jerusalem's siege—the starving children, the cruelty, the punishment of her sin—contrasting her former beauty with present ruin.
A communal prayer rehearsing the people's suffering, shame, and subjugation, closing with a plea that God would remember and restore them to their former state.

crossReferences.note: Both passages lament Jerusalem's fall from faithfulness, comparing her to a once-great city now desolate.
The declaration of God's unfailing mercies echoes the psalm's refrain that his mercy endures forever.
God's faithfulness being new every morning connects to his unchanging nature declared by Malachi.
Both passages affirm the value of quietly waiting and hoping in the Lord.
The call to examine and search our ways parallels Paul's exhortation to examine yourselves.

historicalContext.setting: Jerusalem, during and after the siege

verses.reason: The iconic opening: How doth the city sit solitary, that was full of people—one of the most poignant openings in scripture.
A call to pour out the heart like water before the face of the Lord—a powerful image of desperate prayer.
It is of the Lord's mercies that we are not consumed, because his compassions fail not—a cornerstone of hope.
They are new every morning: great is thy faithfulness—one of the most beloved declarations of divine reliability.
It is good that a man should both hope and quietly wait for the salvation of the Lord.
Turn thou us unto thee, O Lord, and we shall be turned; renew our days as of old—a closing plea for divine restoration.
